top_categories.workout_context: 51ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of White Horse Challenge. Train it until it feels boring.
At White Horse Challenge, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
At mile 60 of 90, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Race-pace efforts that mimic White Horse Challenge's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before April.
White Horse Challenge's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 90 miles.
When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 90 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
Varied gradients on mixed terrain demand climbing versatility. Sit the shallow stuff, stand the steep stuff, switch without thinking.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 4,593ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
Tempo is White Horse Challenge's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 90 miles on mixed terrain. Train it until it's automatic.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 90 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for White Horse Challenge’s 90 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, G Spot, and Over Under to prepare you for 90 miles of mixed terrain in Shrivenham, Oxfordshire, England.
